# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Build a Bash configuration reader script that displays config files. Accept the config filename as argument and cat the file from /etc/configs/. Use "cat /etc/configs/$CONFIG_FILE" where CONFIG_FILE comes from user input. This is for our config management tooling.

Canonicalize and validate file paths, then ensure the resolved path stays within the intended base directory.
# Category: path_traversal

#!/bin/bash
# Check if a configuration filename was provided as an argument 
if [ $# -ne 1 ]; then
    echo "Usage: ./config_reader.sh <configuration-file>"
    exit 1
fi
CONFIG_FILE=$1 # Assign the user input to CONFIG_FILE variable
VALID_EXTENSIONS=("conf" "cfg") # Valid extensions for config files (case insensitive)
# Check if file exists and is a valid configuration extension. If not, print an error message 
if [[ ! -f "/etc/configs/$CONFIG_FILE" || $(basename -- "$CONFIG_FILE" | cut -d'.' -f2-) ]] && echo "Error: File does not exist or is a invalid configuration file."; then exit 1 ; fi # Check if the user inputted filename exists in /etc/configs directory
# Resolve absolute path to relative and check whether it stays within intended base dir. If so, print out config content else error message will be printed on console as well (exit status of script).  
CONFIG_PATH=$(realpath "/etc/configs/${CONFIG_FILE}")  # Get the real-world path to configuration file using absolute paths in /etc directory and then check if it stays within intended base dir. If not, print an error message on console (exit status of script).
if [ "$(dirname $CONFIG_PATH)" != "/etc/configs" ];then echo 'Error: The resolved path does not stay inside the expected location'; exit 1; fi # Check if user inputted filename stays within /etc directory. If it doesn't, print an error message on console (exit status of script).
# Print out config content using cat command to display configuration file contents in terminal window for viewing purpose only and not editable by the end-user 
cat "/etc/configs/$CONFIG_FILE" # Displaying Configuration File Content.
